title: DSA Mastery
description: Master the algorithms and data structures that power modern data platforms.

topics.title: 1. Complexity Analysis
topics.description: Big O Notation (Time & Space), how to analyze loops & recursion.
topics.content:
Understanding Complexity

Complexity analysis is the tool we use to measure how well an algorithm scales. As your data grows from 100 rows to 100 million rows, will your code still run in milliseconds, or will it take hours?

Time Complexity

How the number of operations increases as the input size (N) grows.

Space Complexity

How much extra memory the algorithm needs as the input size (N) grows.

Core Concepts

  • Big O (O): Upper bound (worst-case growth). It tells us the maximum growth rate as input size grows.
  • Big Theta (Θ): Tight bound (average/typical growth when upper and lower bounds match).
  • Big Omega (Ω): Lower bound (best-case growth).
  • Rule of thumb: Ignore constants and lower-order terms (for example, O(3N + 10) becomes O(N)).

Big O Classes with Python Examples

From fastest-growing to slowest-growing runtime, we usually compare algorithms using these classes:

  • O(1) - Constant: Runtime does not depend on input size.
  • O(log N) - Logarithmic: Work reduces by a factor each step (e.g., halving search space).
  • O(N) - Linear: One full pass through input.
  • O(N log N) - Linearithmic: Divide-and-conquer style work (common in efficient sorting).
  • O(N²) - Quadratic: Nested loops over the same input.
  • O(2^N) - Exponential: Branching recursion, doubling possibilities per step.
  • O(N!) - Factorial: All permutations/combinations of ordering explored.

O(1) - Constant Time

Work stays the same no matter how large the input gets.

def first_item(arr):
    return arr[0] if arr else None

O(log N) - Logarithmic Time

Each step discards a large portion of the search space.

def binary_search(arr, target):
    left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while left <= right:
        mid = (left + right) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid
        if arr[mid] < target:
            left = mid + 1
        else:
            right = mid - 1
    return -1

O(N) - Linear Time

You process each element once in the worst case.

def contains_value(arr, target):
    for x in arr:
        if x == target:
            return True
    return False

O(N log N) - Linearithmic Time

Common for efficient sorting and divide-and-conquer strategies.

def sort_values(arr):
    return sorted(arr)

O(N^2) - Quadratic Time

Typical pattern: nested loops over the same input.

def print_pairs(arr):
    for i in arr:
        for j in arr:
            print(i, j)

O(2^N) - Exponential Time

Branching recursion creates rapidly growing repeated work.

def fib(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2)

O(N!) - Factorial Time

Exploring all permutations quickly becomes infeasible as N grows.

def permutations(items):
    if len(items) <= 1:
        return [items]
    result = []
    for i in range(len(items)):
        rest = items[:i] + items[i + 1:]
        for p in permutations(rest):
            result.append([items[i]] + p)
    return result

topics.title: 2. Arrays
topics.description: Array patterns: two pointers, sliding window, prefix sums.
topics.content:
Understanding Arrays

An Array is the most fundamental data structure. It stores elements in contiguous memory locations, meaning they sit right next to each other in your computer's RAM.

graph LR subgraph Array direction LR A["10 (Index 0)"] --- B["20 (Index 1)"] --- C["30 (Index 2)"] --- D["40 (Index 3)"] --- E["50 (Index 4)"] end

Core Operations

  • Access: O(1). Since items are contiguous, we can jump directly to any index.
  • Search: O(N). In an unsorted array, we must check every element one by one.
  • Insertion/Deletion: O(N). When you add or remove an item in the middle, you have to shift all subsequent items to make room or close the gap.

Advanced Techniques

Most array interview questions are variations of a few patterns. If you learn the pattern, you can solve a whole family of problems.

1) Traversal (Single pass)

When to use: You need to compute something over all elements: sum, min/max, counts, frequency of a value, check a condition, build a transformed list.

Key idea: Keep a running answer while scanning left → right. This gives you O(N) time and O(1) extra space (usually).

def count_even(nums):
    count = 0
    for x in nums:
        if x % 2 == 0:
            count += 1
    return count

# Input
nums = [3, 10, 7, 2, 9, 12]

# Output
print(count_even(nums))  # 3  (10, 2, 12)

2) Two Pointers

When to use: Problems involving pairs, reversing, partitioning, or searching in a sorted array. Common signals: “two numbers”, “closest”, “palindrome-like”, “sorted array”, “remove duplicates in-place”.

Key idea: Maintain two indices (often l and r) and move one or both pointers based on a condition. This often converts a nested loop into a single pass.

def two_sum_sorted(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l < r:
        s = nums[l] + nums[r]
        if s == target:
            return (l, r)
        if s < target:
            l += 1
        else:
            r -= 1
    return None

# Input (sorted array)
nums = [1, 2, 4, 7, 11, 15]
target = 15

# Output
print(two_sum_sorted(nums, target))  # (2, 3) because 4 + 11 = 15

3) Sliding Window

When to use: “subarray/substring”, “contiguous”, “largest/smallest window”, “at most / at least K”, “maximum sum/average of size K”.

Key idea: Keep a window [l..r] and update its state when you expand r (add) and shrink l (remove). Avoid recomputing sums/counts from scratch.

Fixed-size window: maximum sum of size K
def max_sum_subarray(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window_sum

    for r in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[r] - nums[r - k]
        best = max(best, window_sum)

    return best

# Input
nums = [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]
k = 3

# Output
print(max_sum_subarray(nums, k))  # 9  (5 + 1 + 3)
Variable-size window: smallest subarray length with sum ≥ S
def min_len_subarray_at_least_s(nums, s):
    l = 0
    window_sum = 0
    best = float('inf')

    for r in range(len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[r]
        while window_sum >= s:
            best = min(best, r - l + 1)
            window_sum -= nums[l]
            l += 1

    return 0 if best == float('inf') else best

# Input
nums = [2, 3, 1, 2, 4, 3]
s = 7

# Output
print(min_len_subarray_at_least_s(nums, s))  # 2  ([4, 3])

4) Prefix Sum

When to use: Many “range sum” queries, subarray sum equals K, “sum between i and j” repeatedly, or “count subarrays with property …”.

Key idea: Build a prefix array where pref[i] = sum of first i elements. Then range sum becomes:

\(\text{sum}(l..r) = \text{pref}[r+1] - \text{pref}[l]\)

Range sum queries in O(1)
def build_prefix(nums):
    pref = [0]
    for x in nums:
        pref.append(pref[-1] + x)
    return pref

def range_sum(pref, l, r):
    return pref[r + 1] - pref[l]

# Input
nums = [5, -2, 3, 1, 2]
pref = build_prefix(nums)

# Output
print(range_sum(pref, 1, 3))  # 2  (-2 + 3 + 1)
print(range_sum(pref, 0, 4))  # 9  (sum of all)
Subarray sum equals K (prefix sum + hashmap)
def count_subarrays_sum_k(nums, k):
    freq = {0: 1}  # prefix_sum -> count
    prefix = 0
    count = 0

    for x in nums:
        prefix += x
        count += freq.get(prefix - k, 0)
        freq[prefix] = freq.get(prefix, 0) + 1

    return count

# Input
nums = [1, 2, 3, -2, 5, -3, 1]
k = 3

# Output
print(count_subarrays_sum_k(nums, k))  # 5

topics.title: 3. Strings
topics.description: String manipulation, pattern matching, and encoding techniques.
topics.content:
String Manipulation

A string is essentially an array of characters. However, in many modern languages like Python, strings are immutable—meaning once they are created, they cannot be changed.

Key Concepts

  • Immutability: In Python, s = "Hello" followed by s += "!" actually creates a brand new string in memory.
  • Slicing: Extracting parts of a string (e.g., s[1:4]) is a core operation.
  • Character Frequency: Using HashMaps to count occurrences is the most common interview pattern.

Common Patterns

  • Palindrome: A word that reads the same forwards and backwards.
  • Anagram: Two strings containing the exact same characters in any order.

Python Example: Palindrome & Anagram

# Check if a string is a palindrome using Two Pointers
def is_palindrome(s):
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        if s[left] != s[right]: return False
        left, right = left + 1, right - 1
    return True

# Check if two strings are anagrams using Counter
from collections import Counter
def is_anagram(s1, s2):
    return Counter(s1) == Counter(s2)

Detailed Concepts (with Code & I/O)

1) Immutability (Transform by creating a new string)

In Python: Strings are immutable. So when you do something like s += "!", Python creates a new string instead of modifying the old one.

Common interview implication: Build the answer incrementally using a list, then join (or use comprehensions/slicing) instead of trying to mutate characters in place.

def reverse_string(s):
    # We create a NEW string (valid because strings are immutable).
    return ''.join(reversed(s))

# Input
s = "data"

# Output
print(reverse_string(s))  # "atad"

2) Slicing & Indexing (extract substrings fast)

Slicing rule: s[l:r] includes index l but excludes index r.

Why it shows up: Many problems ask you to normalize strings (remove prefix/suffix), compare parts, or build substrings.

def extract_part(s):
    # Extract indices [2..5] => s[2:6]
    return s[2:6]

# Input
s = "cakedata"

# Output
print(extract_part(s))  # "keda"

3) Character Frequency (HashMap / Counter pattern)

Use it when: you need to compare counts (anagrams, grouping, “same characters”, isomorphic checks).

def freq_map(s):
    freq = {}
    for ch in s:
        freq[ch] = freq.get(ch, 0) + 1
    return freq

# Input
s = "abracadabra"

# Output
print(freq_map(s)['a'])  # 5

4) Palindrome (Two Pointers)

Idea: Put two pointers at the ends. If characters mismatch, it cannot be a palindrome.

Complexity: O(N) time, O(1) extra space.

def is_palindrome(s):
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        if s[left] != s[right]:
            return False
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return True

# Input
s = "racecar"

# Output
print(is_palindrome(s))  # True

5) Anagram (frequency match)

Definition: Two strings are anagrams if they contain the same characters with the same counts (order doesn’t matter).

from collections import Counter

def is_anagram(s1, s2):
    if len(s1) != len(s2):
        return False
    return Counter(s1) == Counter(s2)

# Input
s1 = "listen"
s2 = "silent"

# Output
print(is_anagram(s1, s2))  # True

topics.title: 4. Hashing
topics.description: Hash maps, hash sets, collision handling, and use cases.
topics.content:
HashMap & HashSet

A HashMap stores Key-Value pairs. It uses a mathematical hashing function to turn a key into an index, allowing for instant O(1) lookups. A HashSet is like a HashMap but only stores unique keys.

flowchart LR Key["Key: 'apple'"] --> HashFunction(Hash Function) HashFunction --> Index[Index: 3] Index --> Memory[Bucket 3: Value=50]

Core Concepts

  • Hashing: The process of converting a key into a fixed-size integer.
  • Collision: When two different keys result in the same hash. Modern dictionaries handle this automatically.
  • O(1) Lookups: Finding data in a hash map doesn't depend on how many items are in it.

The Two-Sum Pattern

# Problem: Find indices of two numbers that add up to target
def two_sum(nums, target):
    seen = {} # Number: Index
    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
        complement = target - num
        if complement in seen:
            return [seen[complement], i]
        seen[num] = i
    return []

print(two_sum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9)) # Output: [0, 1]

Frequency Count Pattern

What it solves: Count occurrences, validate conditions like “same characters”, or group items by value.

Time: O(N) to build the map, space: O(N) in worst case.

def frequency_count(nums):
    freq = {}
    for x in nums:
        freq[x] = freq.get(x, 0) + 1
    return freq

# Input
nums = [1, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1]

# Output
print(frequency_count(nums))  # {1: 3, 2: 2, 3: 1}

HashSet Pattern (unique / presence checks)

Use HashSet when: you only care whether an element exists, or you want to remove duplicates quickly.

Time: O(N) average, with O(N) extra space.

def contains_duplicate(nums):
    seen = set()
    for x in nums:
        if x in seen:
            return True
        seen.add(x)
    return False

# Input
nums = [1, 2, 3, 1]

# Output
print(contains_duplicate(nums))  # True

topics.title: 5. Stacks
topics.description: Stack operations, monotonic stacks, and applications.
topics.content:
Understanding Stacks

A Stack is a collection of elements that follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. Think of a stack of plates: you can only add a plate to the top and take a plate from the top.

flowchart TD subgraph Stack Push["Push (Add)"] --> Top["Top Element"] Top --> Pop["Pop (Remove)"] Top -.-> Peek["Peek (View)"] end

The Three Core Operations

PUSH

Adds an element to the Top of the stack.

POP

Removes the Top element and returns it.

PEEK

Looks at the Top element without removing it.

What problem does it solve?

Stacks are perfect for tracking state that needs to be reversed or unwound. They are the core data structure behind the "Undo" button in text editors, browser history (back button), reversing strings, and verifying matching brackets in compilers.

Python Example: Valid Parentheses

In Python, you can just use a standard list [] as a stack using append() and pop().

# Problem: Check if a string of brackets is valid
def is_valid_parentheses(s):
    stack = []
    mapping = {')': '(', '}': '{', ']': '['}
    
    for char in s:
        if char in mapping:
            # If stack is not empty, pop top element. Else use dummy '#'
            top_element = stack.pop() if stack else '#'
            if mapping[char] != top_element:
                return False
        else:
            # It's an opening bracket, push to stack
            stack.append(char)
            
    return not stack # True if stack is empty

print(is_valid_parentheses("{[()]}")) # Output: True
print(is_valid_parentheses("{[(])}")) # Output: False

Python Example: Reverse a String (Stack)

Use a stack to reverse: push all characters, then pop to rebuild from the top.

def reverse_string_stack(s):
    stack = []
    for ch in s:
        stack.append(ch)  # PUSH

    # Pop in reverse order to rebuild the string.
    out = []
    while stack:
        out.append(stack.pop())  # POP
    return ''.join(out)

# Input
s = "stacks"

# Output
print(reverse_string_stack(s))  # "skcats"

topics.title: 6. Queues
topics.content:
Understanding Queues

A Queue is a collection of elements that follows the FIFO (First In, First Out) principle. Think of a line at a coffee shop: you join the back of the line and get served from the front.

flowchart LR subgraph Queue direction LR Enqueue["Enqueue (Add)"] --> Back["Back"] Back --> Front["Front"] Front --> Dequeue["Dequeue (Remove)"] end

Core Operations

ENQUEUE

Adds an element to the Back of the queue.

DEQUEUE

Removes the Front element (the one that was there the longest).

What problem does it solve?

Queues are used for scheduling and ordering tasks fairly. If multiple users want to print a document, the printer uses a queue. They are also heavily used in web server requests, task scheduling (like Celery/RabbitMQ), and algorithms like Breadth-First Search (BFS).

Python Example: Basic Queue using Deque

In Python, while you could use a list, removing from the front of a list is very slow O(N). Instead, we use collections.deque which makes adding and removing from both ends instant O(1).

from collections import deque

def simulate_printer_queue():
    # Initialize a queue
    printer_queue = deque()
    
    # Enqueue (Add to back)
    print("Adding jobs to queue...")
    printer_queue.append("Job 1: Resume.pdf")
    printer_queue.append("Job 2: Invoice.docx")
    printer_queue.append("Job 3: Photo.png")
    
    # Dequeue (Remove from front)
    print("Processing jobs:")
    while printer_queue: # While queue is not empty
        current_job = printer_queue.popleft() # O(1) removal
        print(f"Printing -> {current_job}")

simulate_printer_queue()
# Output:
# Printing -> Job 1: Resume.pdf
# Printing -> Job 2: Invoice.docx
# Printing -> Job 3: Photo.png

Python Example: Breadth-First Search (BFS)

Why queues: BFS explores nodes level-by-level. The queue holds the “next layer” to process.

Complexity: O(V + E) time for a graph with V vertices and E edges.

from collections import deque

def bfs_levels(graph, start):
    # graph: dict[node] -> list of neighbors
    visited = set([start])
    q = deque([(start, 0)])  # (node, level)
    levels = {}

    while q:
        node, level = q.popleft()
        levels[node] = level

        for nei in graph.get(node, []):
            if nei not in visited:
                visited.add(nei)
                q.append((nei, level + 1))

    return levels

# Input
graph = {
    0: [1, 2],
    1: [3],
    2: [3, 4],
    3: [],
    4: []
}
start = 0

# Output
print(bfs_levels(graph, start))
    # {0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 1, 3: 2, 4: 2}

topics.title: 7. Linked List
topics.description: Linked list traversal, reversal, and fast/slow pointer patterns.
topics.content:
Understanding Linked Lists

Unlike arrays where data is stored contiguously, a Linked List is made of independent Nodes. Each node is an object that contains data and a "link" (pointer) to the next object.

graph LR Head[Node: 10] --> Node2[Node: 20] Node2 --> Node3[Node: 30] Node3 --> Null[None]

Core Anatomy

  • Node: The basic building block containing data and next.
  • Head: The first node in the list. If you lose this, you lose the whole list!
  • Pointer: The memory address of the next node.

What problem does it solve?

In arrays, inserting at the beginning is slow O(N). In a Linked List, inserting at the beginning is instant O(1) because you just change a pointer.

Python Example: Reversing a Linked List

class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

def reverse_list(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = prev # Reverse pointer
        prev, curr = curr, next_node
    return prev

topics.title: 8. Binary Search
topics.description: Binary search on sorted arrays and search spaces.
topics.content:
Binary Search

Binary Search is a remarkably efficient algorithm for finding an item from a sorted list of items. It works by repeatedly dividing in half the portion of the list that could contain the item.

Requirement: Sorted Data

Binary Search ONLY works if your data is already sorted. If not, the "divide and conquer" logic fails.

Core Concepts

  • Midpoint: Calculated as (left + right) // 2.
  • Divide & Conquer: Each step eliminates half of the remaining elements.
  • Time Complexity: O(log N). Incredibly fast for massive datasets.

Python Example: Binary Search

def binary_search(arr, target):
    left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while left <= right:
        mid = (left + right) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target: return mid
        elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid + 1
        else: right = mid - 1
    return -1

topics.title: 9. Sorting
topics.description: Merge sort, quicksort, and sorting algorithm analysis.
topics.content:
Sorting Algorithms

Sorting is the process of arranging data in a specific order. While production code uses built-in sorts, understanding the concepts is vital.

Algorithm Time Stability
Merge Sort O(N log N) Stable (Preserves order of equal items)
Quick Sort O(N log N) Unstable

Core Concepts

  • Stability: Does the sort preserve the relative order of duplicate elements?
  • In-place: Does the sort require extra memory or does it sort within the original array?
  • Divide & Conquer: Breaking the array into single items and merging them back (Merge Sort).

Python Example: Timsort

# Python's built-in sort is highly optimized
nums = [5, 2, 9, 1, 5, 6]
nums.sort() # In-place sort
print(nums) # [1, 2, 5, 5, 6, 9]

topics.title: 10. Recursion
topics.description: Recursion patterns, memoization, and dynamic programming intro.
topics.content:
What is Recursion?

Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller versions of the same problem.

The Two Essential Rules:

  1. Base Case: The condition that stops the recursion. Without it, you get a Stack Overflow.
  2. Recursive Case: The part where the function calls itself with a smaller input.

Related Concepts

  • Call Stack: The memory area where your computer tracks the nested function calls.
  • Recursive Tree: A visual map of all the function calls generated.

Python Example: Factorial

def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1: return 1 # Base Case
    return n * factorial(n - 1) # Recursive Case

topics.title: 11. Trees
topics.description: Binary trees, BST, DFS/BFS traversal, and tree problems.
topics.content:
Understanding Trees

A Tree is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes. The topmost node is the Root.

graph TD A((Root: 10)) --> B((Child: 5)) A --> C((Child: 15)) B --> D((Leaf: 3)) B --> E((Leaf: 7))

Core Anatomy

  • Parent/Child: A node that points to another is a Parent; the node pointed to is a Child.
  • Leaf: A node with no children (the "ends" of the tree).
  • Depth/Height: Measures of how "deep" or "tall" the tree is.

Tree Traversal

  • DFS (Inorder): Visit Left, Root, Right. Used in Binary Search Trees to get sorted data.
  • BFS: Visit level by level. Used for shortest path in hierarchical structures.

Python Example: Inorder DFS

def inorder(root):
    if not root: return []
    return inorder(root.left) + [root.val] + inorder(root.right)

topics.title: 12. Graphs
topics.description: Graph representations, BFS/DFS, shortest path algorithms.
topics.content:
Understanding Graphs

A Graph is a network of Nodes (Vertices) connected by Edges.

graph LR A((A)) --- B((B)) B --- C((C)) A --- C C --> D((D))

Core Anatomy

  • Vertex: A single node in the graph.
  • Edge: The connection between two vertices.
  • Directed vs Undirected: Does the edge have a direction (like a follow) or is it mutual (like a friend)?

Representation

Graphs are usually stored as an Adjacency List (a dictionary where keys are nodes and values are lists of neighbors).

Python Example: Simple DFS

def dfs(graph, node, visited=set()):
    if node not in visited:
        print(node)
        visited.add(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            dfs(graph, neighbor, visited)

topics.title: 13. Heap
topics.description: Min/max heaps, priority queues, and top-K problems.
topics.content:
Understanding Heaps

A Heap is a tree-based data structure that satisfies the Heap Property. It is primarily used for priority-based tasks.

graph TD A((Min: 1)) --> B((3)) A --> C((6)) B --> D((5)) B --> E((9))

Core Concepts

  • Min Heap: Smallest element is at the Root.
  • Max Heap: Largest element is at the Root.
  • Heapify: The process of rearranging a tree to satisfy the heap property.
  • Extract Min/Max: Removing the root and "re-heapifying" the tree in O(log N).

Python Example: Finding Top-K Largest

import heapq
def k_largest(nums, k):
    return heapq.nlargest(k, nums)

print(k_largest([3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], 2)) # [6, 5]
